description: Primary-sourced UX-writing/microcopy rules — clarity, voice, error/empty/button copy, and scannability. Re-derived from NN/g UX-writing research and the plainlanguage.gov guidelines.

rules.title: Plain, concise language
rules.rule: Use short common words, active voice, and short sentences; cut filler. Write for the user's task, not the org chart. Aim for a low reading grade for broad audiences.

rules.title: Buttons name the action, not 'OK/Submit'
rules.rule: Button labels state the specific outcome (verb + object) so the choice is clear out of context. Avoid generic 'Submit'/'OK'/'Yes'.

rules.title: Errors: what happened + how to fix
rules.rule: Error microcopy states the problem in plain terms, avoids blame, and gives a concrete next step. No raw codes, no 'invalid input'.

rules.title: Empty states teach + invite action
rules.rule: Empty-state copy explains what goes here and offers the first action; don't leave a blank panel. Turn the void into onboarding.
rules.do: "No projects yet — create your first." + CTA.

rules.title: Front-load + make text scannable
rules.rule: Users scan, not read — put the key info first (inverted pyramid), use meaningful headings, bullets, and bolded keywords. One idea per paragraph.

source.name: NN/g — How users read on the web

rules.title: Consistent terminology + voice
rules.rule: Use one term per concept everywhere (not 'delete' here, 'remove' there); keep a consistent, human voice and tone calibrated to context (calm in errors).
